nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; found under rocky overhangs, in caves with sponges, and beneath large plate corals in sheltered inner reefs and tide pools

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs

diet.notes: Primarily targets live copepods and zooplankton; in the wild has been documented acting as a cleaner fish, removing parasites and dead tissue from other reef species. In captivity, generally advised to provide a steady supply of live copepods from a mature reef or pod culture. Has been reported to accept frozen Mysis, Cyclop-Eeze, Nutramar Ova, and vitamin-enriched baby brine shrimp once acclimated, though live foods tend to be strongly preferred, especially initially. Captive-bred individuals tend to accept prepared foods more readily.

summary: A slender, expert-level saltwater pipefish with a vivid orange body and blue head, generally kept in species-only or seahorse tanks. Primarily feeds on live copepods and demands a mature, low-flow reef with ample live rock.
careNotes:
Janss' Pipefish is generally considered one of the more approachable pipefish for experienced reef keepers, though it remains classified as expert-only by most retailers due to its specialized feeding requirements. It has been known to exhibit cleaner fish behavior in aquariums, visiting other tank inhabitants to remove ectoparasites — a behavior observed in the wild where it maintains established cleaning stations frequented by cardinalfish and damselfish.

This species tends to prefer low water flow and is generally advised to be housed away from powerheads and strong circulation. It spends most of its time swimming in the mid-water column beneath overhangs and among live rock, rarely resting on the substrate. Males carry eggs in a brood pouch located under the tail (ovoviviparous reproduction).

Captive-bred individuals have been reported to live up to 5 years with proper care; wild-caught specimens often fare more poorly, with lifespan estimates of 2–3 years more common in the literature. A species-only or seahorse-dominated setup is strongly recommended to eliminate competition for food from faster, more aggressive feeders.

Temperature disagreement across sources was noted: LiveAquaria recommends 72–78°F (22–26°C), while FishBase records wild observations at 25.9–29.3°C and TankFacts recommends 76–80°F (24–27°C). The value used here (24–27°C) represents an average across sources. ReefApp listed a minimum tank size of 100 L, which is considered too small for this expert species given its need for stable water chemistry and open swimming space; 200 L is used per the cautious rounding rule. ReefApp described the species as 'always reef safe', while LiveAquaria rates it 'with caution' due to vulnerability to stinging corals and anemones — the more protective classification is used here.
breedingNotes: Males brood eggs in a ventral pouch beneath the tail. Breeding in captivity has been reported but is considered difficult; captive-bred specimens are occasionally available from specialist breeders. The species is ovoviviparous.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (confirmed 14.0 cm TL), native range, temperature range (wild: 25.9–29.3°C), and taxonomy. pH and salinity not stated on FishBase summary page.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (14 cm), temperature (~26°C), salinity (~1.026), and tank size (100 L — considered conservative understatement for this species; 200 L used). Lifespan given as 2–3 years.

additional.name: TankFacts
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (76–80°F / 24.4–26.7°C), pH (7.8–8.4), salinity (1.023–1.026), and kH (8–12 dKH).

fish.breedingNotes: Males carry eggs in a ventral brood pouch (ovoviviparous). Captive breeding is occasionally achieved by specialist keepers; captive-bred specimens are available from some dedicated breeders but are uncommon.
